text: Metal-organic frameworks-derived nonprecious dual metal single-atom catalysts for efficient oxygen reduction reaction The use of hydrogen fuel cells as the next generation of power sources for the future automobiles has drawn increasing attention. The efficient, stable and low-cost catalyst for fuel cells is still a bottleneck for the commercialization of fuel cells, since it has to catalyze the sluggish oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) with fast kinetics. In this work, the novel nonprecious dual metal single-atom catalysts (SACs) with high performance and stability were synthesized by adjusting the contents of Zn and Co in metal-organic frameworks. After 50k cycles, the SACs showed a half-wave potential (E1/2) of 0.938 V vs RHE and a small Tafel slope of 59.4 mV with a small ΔE1/2 of -8.5 mV, exceeding most of reported catalysts. More interestingly, the SACs exhibited a peak power density of as high as 1 W cm-2 at 0.8 V under the working conditions in fuel cells, which ranked the highest value of SACs for alkaline fuel cells reported so far. Meanwhile, operando X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) was used to clarify the catalytically active species and provide the catalytic mechanism of the SACs. It was confirmed that the Co single atom at Co-N4 sites was the main catalytically active species, where one axial oxygen was bonded on the surface of the Oads-Co-N4 moiety during the ORR process. Finally, potential-dependent microkinetic model based on DFT calculations and core-level shift calculations were further confirmed the presence of axial oxygenated species bonding on the Co-N4 sites during the ORR. In summary, this study provides a deep insight into the catalytic mechanism of the SACs, and we believe this work is beneficial for the future design and development of single-atom catalysts for efficient fuel cells and other energy-related applications.
